content:
Researchers have long **simulated variation** to teach invariance. **Tangent distance** and **virtual examples** in the 1990s showed that **synthetic deformations** of character images improved generalization without new labels — the same principle as modern augmentation.

**Convolutional neural networks** for document and digit recognition routinely used **shift, scale, and elastic deformations** in training (LeCun et al., 1990s). **ImageNet** training pipelines popularized **random crop and horizontal flip** as default regularizers for deep vision.

**SMOTE** (Chawla et al., 2002) addressed **class imbalance** in tabular data by **interpolating synthetic minority samples** — augmentation in feature space rather than pixel space.

**NLP** adopted **back-translation** and **EDA (Easy Data Augmentation)** in the 2010s to stretch small labeled text sets for classification and NLI.

**AutoAugment** and learned augmentation policies (2018+) search over transform sequences — beyond this beginner topic, but rooted in the same **label-preserving diversity** goal for **overfitting mitigation**.

timeline.label: Virtual examples & deformations (1990s)
timeline.description: Handwritten character and document CNNs train on transformed copies to improve robustness without new labels.

timeline.label: SMOTE for imbalanced tabular data (2002)
timeline.description: Synthetic minority oversampling adds feature-space neighbors to reduce majority-class overfitting.

timeline.label: ImageNet-scale random crop & flip (2010s)
timeline.description: Deep vision pipelines standardize on-the-fly spatial augmentation during large-scale training.

timeline.label: Text back-translation & EDA (2010s)
timeline.description: Paraphrase-style augmentation expands small NLP training sets while preserving labels when validated.

timeline.label: Learned augmentation policies (2018+)
timeline.description: AutoAugment and successors search transform sequences — evolution of hand-picked regularization.
